subject: anatomy-1
title: [FIXTURE] Upper Limb
description: Fixture topic test exercising mcq + tf, image and text explanations, and both yield tiers.

questions.stem: A patient cannot abduct the arm through the 15°–90° mid-range, and the rounded contour of the shoulder is flattened. Which nerve is most likely injured?

explanation.imageAlt: Diagram of the deltoid and its axillary nerve supply over the shoulder.
explanation.caption: The deltoid drives mid-range abduction (15–90°) and is supplied by the axillary nerve; injury flattens the shoulder contour.
explanation.text: The deltoid is the prime mover for abduction between 15° and 90° and is innervated by the axillary nerve (C5–C6).

questions.stem: Which muscle initiates abduction of the arm over the first 0–15° before the deltoid takes over?

explanation.text: Supraspinatus initiates the first ~15° of abduction; the deltoid then dominates the mid-range. Both are commonly tested together.

questions.stem: The musculocutaneous nerve supplies the biceps brachii.

explanation.text: True. The musculocutaneous nerve (C5–C7) innervates the anterior compartment of the arm — biceps brachii, brachialis, and coracobrachialis.

questions.stem: Which muscle is the workhorse flexor of the elbow regardless of forearm position, since it inserts on the ulna and is unaffected by pronation/supination?

explanation.text: Brachialis flexes the elbow in every forearm position because it inserts on the ulnar tuberosity. The biceps loses leverage when the forearm is pronated.

questions.stem: A patient develops a winged scapula after axillary lymph-node surgery. Which nerve was most likely injured?

explanation.text: The long thoracic nerve supplies serratus anterior; its loss un-anchors the scapula, producing medial winging. It is vulnerable during axillary dissection.
